title: Effects of a cultural reconciliation procedure on multinational collaborative decision making
author: Rasmussen, Louise J.
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Collaborative decision making in culturally diverse teams is common in a variety of organizational settings. Individuals from different cultural backgrounds bring with them unique knowledge and perspectives that, when brought together, should yield ideas and problem solutions that are more creative and of higher quality than culturally homogeneous teams. However, research has shown that cultural diversity in teams often leads to decreased performance. The purpose of the current study is to examine the effects of reconciling cultural differences on multinational collaborative decision-making. Two groups of culturally diverse teams completed both an idea-generation task and a rank ordering task. Both tasks were completed in a distributed fashion using a web phone. Half the groups engaged in a cultural reconciliation procedure prior to completing the experimental tasks, the other half engaged in a control activity. The results indicated that the groups that had experienced the reconciliation procedure performed significantly worse on the idea-generation and rank ordering tasks, and they experienced lower overall team efficacy. The results suggest that future research should examine procedures and reconciliation concepts calling attention to cultural commonalities between team members, rather than differences. Theoretical and practical implications are discussed.
